Arguments.prompt: Query the Microsoft Planetary Computer STAC API for the MODIS MOD13A1 version 061 collection. Dynamically determine today's date and identify the 12 most recent complete calendar months (i.e., months that have fully elapsed before the current month). For each of those 12 months, construct a temporal filter spanning the first to the last day of that month. Apply a spatial bounding box filter corresponding to the state of Oregon (approximately longitude -124.6 to -116.5, latitude 41.9 to 46.3). For each month, retrieve all available scene items and request the '500m_16_days_NDVI' asset from each item. Load the raster data for all scenes within a given month and mosaic or aggregate them into a single monthly composite raster using a mean or median approach. After mosaicking, apply the required scale factor: divide all pixel values by 10,000 to convert the packed integer NDVI values to true floating-point NDVI in the range -1 to 1. Mask or exclude any fill values, no-data pixels, or out-of-range values (i.e., scaled values outside -1 to 1) so that only valid NDVI observations remain. Retain the coordinate reference system metadata from the source rasters. Produce a single serialized data object (e.g., a multi-band GeoTIFF or a collection of single-band GeoTIFFs, one per month) containing all 12 scaled and masked monthly NDVI composites, clearly labeled or ordered by calendar month in chronological sequence. Save this output so it is available to downstream actions via the data registry.

Retrieve the Oregon state boundary polygon from the United States Census Bureau. A suitable source is the Census Bureau's TIGER/Line or Cartographic Boundary shapefiles for US states (e.g., the generalized 1:20,000,000 or 1:5,000,000 scale state boundaries available at https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2023/shp/ or equivalent year). Filter the downloaded dataset to retain only the polygon corresponding to Oregon. Additionally, retrieve county-level boundary polygons for Oregon from the same Census Bureau source (e.g., the national county boundary shapefile filtered to Oregon's FIPS state code 41). Save the Oregon state boundary and Oregon county boundaries as separate vector files (e.g., GeoJSON or shapefile format) so they are available to downstream actions via the data registry. Do not reproject at this stage; preserve the original coordinate reference system of the downloaded boundaries.

Using the 12 scaled monthly NDVI composite rasters and the Oregon state and county boundary vector files produced by the previous steps, create a publication-quality map grid figure. Discover all available input files from the registry at runtime: locate the monthly NDVI raster data (12 composites, one per calendar month in chronological order) and the Oregon state boundary and county boundary vector files. Before any spatial operations, reproject the state boundary and county boundary vectors to match the coordinate reference system of the NDVI rasters. Clip or mask each monthly NDVI raster to the Oregon state boundary so that only pixels within Oregon are displayed. Arrange the 12 monthly panels in a 4-column by 3-row grid layout in strict chronological order (earliest month first, reading left to right, top to bottom). For each panel, render the spatially distributed NDVI values using the 'YlGn' colormap. All 12 panels must share a single common colorbar with a consistent value range spanning the global minimum and maximum valid NDVI values observed across all 12 composites; position this shared colorbar prominently along the bottom or right side of the overall figure. Overlay the Oregon state boundary and county boundaries on every panel using thin lines in a neutral color such as white or light gray. Title each panel with the abbreviated month name and the four-digit year (e.g., 'Jan 2024') formatted consistently. Remove all axis tick marks, tick labels, and axis spines from every panel to produce a clean cartographic appearance. Render the overall figure at 200 dots per inch with a 16-by-9 aspect ratio. Save the completed figure as a PNG file and upload it to the data registry so it is stored in Amazon S3.

Using the 12 scaled monthly NDVI composite rasters and the Oregon state boundary vector file produced by the previous steps, create a publication-quality time series line plot. Discover all available input files from the registry at runtime: locate the monthly NDVI raster data (12 composites, one per calendar month in chronological order) and the Oregon state boundary vector file. Before any spatial operations, reproject the state boundary vector to match the coordinate reference system of the NDVI rasters. For each monthly composite, clip or mask the raster to the Oregon state boundary and compute the mean NDVI value across all valid (non-masked) pixels within Oregon. Collect the 12 mean NDVI values in chronological order, paired with their corresponding month labels (abbreviated month name and four-digit year, e.g., 'Jan 2024'). Plot these 12 values as a line plot with the months on the horizontal axis in chronological order and mean NDVI on the vertical axis. Style the line clearly (e.g., a solid line with markers at each data point). Identify seasonal peaks (local maxima) and seasonal troughs (local minima) among the 12 values. Annotate each identified peak and trough directly on the plot by marking the point with a distinct marker and labeling it with the corresponding month abbreviation and rounded NDVI value. Include a descriptive title such as 'Mean Monthly NDVI for Oregon', label the horizontal axis as 'Month' and the vertical axis as 'Mean NDVI', and apply a clean grid or background style that aids readability. Save the completed figure as a PNG file at an appropriate resolution (at least 150 dpi) and upload it to the data registry so it is stored in Amazon S3.
